prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
I now have everything I need to give you a complete, precise diagnosis. Here it is.

---

## Diagnosis

**The live site still shows the old content because the route file was never executed.** The content in `route.ts` is not read at build time or on page load — it is the *body* of a Next.js API route that only runs when that endpoint receives an HTTP `POST` request. Saving the file edits the source of the push script; it does not push anything to Payload CMS's database, which is what actually serves the content.

The full causal chain:

```
route.ts (source file, edited ✅)
    │
    │  ← must be triggered manually via HTTP POST
    ▼
Payload CMS database (still has old content ❌)
    │
    │  ← site reads from here at request time
    ▼
Live site (shows old content ❌)
```

---

## Root Cause

This is **CAP-016 — Conflating the content store with the content push script.** The `update-*` routes are one-shot seed/update scripts disguised as API routes. They are the *mechanism that writes to the database*, not the *source of truth that the site reads from*. Editing `BLURB_BODY` or `CONTENT_BLOCKS` in the file only changes what *will be written* on the next invocation — the database row is untouched until `POST /api/update-etro` is actually called.

The production guard in every sibling route confirms this is intentional and load-bearing:

```ts
// update-lacework/route.ts, update-meteor/route.ts (same pattern)
export async function POST() {
  if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production') {
    return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Disabled in production' }, { status: 403 })
  }
  // ... payload.update() / payload.create()
```

The route is a dev-only write tool. The database is the live source of truth.

---

## Fix (Right Now)

With your dev server running, fire the `POST` from a terminal:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/update-etro
```

Or from a browser dev-tools console on any `localhost:3000` page:

```js
fetch('/api/update-etro', { method: 'POST' })
  .then(r => r.json())
  .then(console.log)
```

You should see a response like:

```json
{ "action": "updated", "id": "...", "slug": "etro-framework", "url": "/work/etro-framework" }
```

That confirms Payload has written the new blurb, scope statement, and section bodies from the file into the database. Hard-refresh `/work/etro-framework` and the new content will be live.

---

## The Correct Workflow for Every Content Session

Here is the repeatable three-step loop, matching what the other sibling routes already enforce:

### Step 1 — Edit the source file
Make your prose changes in `route.ts` (`BLURB_BODY`, `SCOPE_STATEMENT`, `CONTENT_BLOCKS`, etc.). This is the single-source-of-truth for the essay's intended state and belongs in version control.

### Step 2 — Push to the database
```bash
# From your project root, with `pnpm dev` running:
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/update-etro
```
The route calls `payload.update()` (or `payload.create()` on first run) against the `projects` collection. Until this runs, the database has not changed.

### Step 3 — Verify in the browser
Navigate to `http://localhost:3000/work/etro-framework`. Because Next.js reads content from Payload at request time, you will immediately see the updated content — no build step needed in dev.

---

## One Additional Caution: Hero Image Preservation

The Meteor route has a `extractHeroImageId()` guard that reads the existing hero block before overwriting, so an uploaded hero image isn't nulled out on re-seed (referenced as EAP-087 in those comments). Check whether the ETRO route has the same guard. If it doesn't and you've already uploaded a hero image in the Payload admin, the `POST` will replace the `hero` block with a `placeholderLabel` block and wipe the image reference. Add the same pattern from `update-meteor/route.ts` before invoking if you've set a hero image:

```ts
// At the top of POST() in update-etro/route.ts, before building content:
const existingDoc = existing.docs[0]
const heroImageId = extractHeroImageId(existingDoc)   // copy helper from meteor route
const data = buildEtroData(heroImageId)               // pass through to createCaseStudyBlocks
```

---

## Principle

> **The `update-*` routes are write scripts, not config files. The database is the live source of truth. Editing the script changes what the next push will write; only the push changes what the site shows.**

The mental model to lock in: treat `route.ts` exactly like a migration file in a Rails project. Editing `db/migrate/20240101_add_columns.rb` does nothing until you run `rails db:migrate`. Same here — editing the route does nothing until you `POST` it.
